(French: [[ʒɑ̃ ɔnɔʁe fʁaɡɔnaʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); 5 April 1732[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-birth_certificate-2) – 22 August 1806) was a French painter and [printmaker](/wiki/Printmaker "Printmaker") whose late [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") manner was distinguished by remarkable facility, exuberance, and [hedonism](/wiki/Hedonism "Hedonism"). One of the most prolific artists active in the last decades of the [Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime"), Fragonard produced more than 550 paintings (not counting drawings and [etchings](/wiki/Etching "Etching")), of which only five are dated. Among his most popular works are [genre paintings](/wiki/Genre_painting "Genre painting") conveying an atmosphere of intimacy and veiled [eroticism](/wiki/Eroticism "Eroticism").

French Rococo painter (1732–1806)

**Jean-Honoré Fragonard** (French: [[ʒɑ̃ ɔnɔʁe fʁaɡɔnaʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); 5 April 1732[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-birth_certificate-2) – 22 August 1806) was a French painter and [printmaker](/wiki/Printmaker "Printmaker") whose late [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") manner was distinguished by remarkable facility, exuberance, and [hedonism](/wiki/Hedonism "Hedonism"). One of the most prolific artists active in the last decades of the [Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime"), Fragonard produced more than 550 paintings (not counting drawings and [etchings](/wiki/Etching "Etching")), of which only five are dated. Among his most popular works are [genre paintings](/wiki/Genre_painting "Genre painting") conveying an atmosphere of intimacy and veiled [eroticism](/wiki/Eroticism "Eroticism").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean-Honor%C3%A9_Fragonard&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/FragonardGrasse.jpg/250px-FragonardGrasse.jpg)](/wiki/File:FragonardGrasse.jpg)

Statue of Fragonard in [Grasse](/wiki/Grasse "Grasse"), his birthplace

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Jean-Honor%C3%A9_Fragonard_-_Cabra-cega.jpg/250px-Jean-Honor%C3%A9_Fragonard_-_Cabra-cega.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Honor%C3%A9_Fragonard_-_Cabra-cega.jpg)

Jean-Honoré Fragonard, *[Blindman's Buff](/wiki/Blind_Man%27s_Buff_(Fragonard,_1775%E2%80%931780) "Blind Man's Buff (Fragonard, 1775–1780)")*, 1775–1780, [Timken Museum of Art](/wiki/Timken_Museum_of_Art "Timken Museum of Art"), San Diego

Jean-Honoré Fragonard was born in [Grasse](/wiki/Grasse "Grasse"), [Alpes-Maritimes](/wiki/Alpes-Maritimes "Alpes-Maritimes"), [France](/wiki/France "France") the only child of François Fragonard, a glover, and Françoise Petit.[[3]](#cite_note-histoire_en_marche-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Harrison-4) In 1738 the family relocated to Paris. The young Fragonard showed an inclination for art, which was recognized by [François Boucher](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher "François Boucher"), who recommended him to the [atelier](/wiki/Atelier "Atelier") of [Chardin](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste-Sim%C3%A9on_Chardin "Jean-Baptiste-Siméon Chardin").[[4]](#cite_note-Harrison-4) Fragonard studied for a short time with Chardin then returned ca. 1749 to Boucher, whose style he soon acquired so completely that the master entrusted him with the execution of replicas of his paintings.[[4]](#cite_note-Harrison-4)

Though not yet a student of the [Academy](/wiki/French_Academy_in_Rome "French Academy in Rome"), Fragonard gained the [Prix de Rome](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome "Prix de Rome") in 1752 with a painting of *[Jeroboam Sacrificing to Idols](/wiki/Jeroboam_Sacrificing_to_Idols "Jeroboam Sacrificing to Idols")*, but before proceeding to Rome he continued to study for three years under [Charles-André van Loo](/wiki/Charles-Andr%C3%A9_van_Loo "Charles-André van Loo").[[4]](#cite_note-Harrison-4) In the year preceding his departure he painted the *Christ washing the Feet of the Apostles* now at [Grasse Cathedral](/wiki/Grasse_Cathedral "Grasse Cathedral"). In December 1756, he took up his abode at the [French Academy in Rome](/wiki/French_Academy_in_Rome "French Academy in Rome"), then presided over by [Charles-Joseph Natoire](/wiki/Charles-Joseph_Natoire "Charles-Joseph Natoire").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911772-5)[[4]](#cite_note-Harrison-4)

While at Rome, Fragonard contracted a friendship with a fellow painter [Hubert Robert](/wiki/Hubert_Robert "Hubert Robert").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1760, they toured Italy together, executing numerous sketches of local scenery. It was in these romantic gardens, with their fountains, grottoes, temples and terraces, that Fragonard conceived the dreams which he was subsequently to render in his art. He also learned to admire the masters of the Dutch and Flemish schools ([Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens"), [Hals](/wiki/Frans_Hals "Frans Hals"), [Rembrandt](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt"), [Ruisdael](/wiki/Jacob_van_Ruisdael "Jacob van Ruisdael")), imitating their loose and vigorous brushstrokes. Added to this influence was the deep impression made upon his mind by the florid sumptuousness of [Giovanni Battista Tiepolo](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Tiepolo "Giovanni Battista Tiepolo"), whose works he had an opportunity to study in Venice before he returned to Paris in 1761.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911773-7)

In 1765 his *[Coresus Sacrificing Himself to Save Callirhoe](/wiki/Coresus_Sacrificing_Himself_to_Save_Callirhoe "Coresus Sacrificing Himself to Save Callirhoe")* secured his admission to the Academy. It was made the subject of a pompous (though not wholly serious) eulogy by [Denis Diderot](/wiki/Denis_Diderot "Denis Diderot"), and was bought by the king, who had it reproduced at the [Gobelins](/wiki/Gobelins_manufactory "Gobelins manufactory") factory. Until this time Fragonard had hesitated between religious, classic and other subjects, but he did not aspire to become a [history painter](/wiki/History_painter "History painter") and he had difficulty completing official commissions. As a consequence, he painted mostly for private patrons or for himself.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The demand of the wealthy art patrons of [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV")'s pleasure-loving and licentious court turned him definitely towards those scenes of love and voluptuousness, which are only made acceptable by the tender beauty of his color and the virtuosity of his facile brushwork; such works include the *[Blind Man's Bluff](/wiki/Blind_Man%27s_Bluff_(Fragonard,_1750) "Blind Man's Bluff (Fragonard, 1750)")* (*Le collin maillard*),[[9]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-9) *Serment d'amour* (Love Vow), *Le Verrou* ([The Bolt](/wiki/The_Bolt_(Fragonard) "The Bolt (Fragonard)")), *La Culbute* (The Tumble), *La Chemise enlevée* ([The Raised Chemise](/wiki/The_Raised_Chemise "The Raised Chemise")), and *[L'escarpolette](/wiki/The_Swing_(Fragonard) "The Swing (Fragonard)")* (The Swing, [Wallace Collection](/wiki/Wallace_Collection "Wallace Collection")), and his decorations for the apartments of [Mme du Barry](/wiki/Madame_du_Barry "Madame du Barry") and the dancer [Madeleine Guimard](/wiki/Madeleine_Guimard "Madeleine Guimard").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911773-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Chaumi%C3%A9re_Italienne.jpg/250px-Chaumi%C3%A9re_Italienne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chaumi%C3%A9re_Italienne.jpg)

Early engraving after Jean-Honoré Fragonard titled *Chaumiére Italienne*[[10]](#cite_note-10)

A lukewarm response to these series of ambitious works induced Fragonard to abandon the Rococo style and to experiment with [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism"). He married [Marie-Anne Gérard](/wiki/Marie-Anne_Fragonard "Marie-Anne Fragonard") (1745–1823), herself a painter of miniatures,[[11]](#cite_note-Monsieur_Fragonard-11) on 17 June 1769 and had a daughter, [Rosalie Fragonard](/w/index.php?title=Rosalie_Fragonard&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rosalie Fragonard (page does not exist)") (1769–1788), who became one of his favourite models. In October 1773, he again went to Italy with Pierre-Jacques Onézyme Bergeret de Grancourt and his son, Pierre-Jacques Bergeret de Grancourt. In September 1774, he returned through [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague"), [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden"), [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") and [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Back in Paris [Marguerite Gérard](/wiki/Marguerite_G%C3%A9rard "Marguerite Gérard"), his wife's 14-year-old sister, became his student and assistant in 1778. In 1780, he had a son, [Alexandre-Évariste Fragonard](/wiki/Alexandre-%C3%89variste_Fragonard "Alexandre-Évariste Fragonard") (1780–1850), who eventually became a talented painter and sculptor. The [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") deprived Fragonard of his private patrons: they were either [guillotined](/wiki/Guillotine "Guillotine") or exiled. The neglected painter deemed it prudent to leave Paris in 1790 and found shelter in the house of his cousin [Alexandre Maubert](/w/index.php?title=Alexandre_Maubert&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alexandre Maubert (page does not exist)") at Grasse, which he decorated with the series of decorative panels known as the *Les progrès de l'amour dans le cœur d'une jeune fille*,[[12]](#cite_note-12) originally painted for [Château du Barry](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_du_Barry "Château du Barry").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Fragonard_-_swing.jpg/250px-Fragonard_-_swing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fragonard_-_swing.jpg)

*[The Swing](/wiki/The_Swing_(Fragonard) "The Swing (Fragonard)")* (French: *L'escarpolette*), 1767, [Wallace Collection](/wiki/Wallace_Collection "Wallace Collection"), London

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean-Honor%C3%A9_Fragonard&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Legacy")]

For half a century or more, Fragonard was so completely ignored that [Wilhelm Lübke](/wiki/Wilhelm_L%C3%BCbke "Wilhelm Lübke")'s 1873 art history volume omits mention of his name.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Later re-evaluations have re-identified his position among the all-time masters of French painting. The influence of his handling of local colour and expressive, confident brushstroke on the [Impressionists](/wiki/Impressionists "Impressionists") (particularly his grand niece, [Berthe Morisot](/wiki/Berthe_Morisot "Berthe Morisot"), and [Renoir](/wiki/Pierre-Auguste_Renoir "Pierre-Auguste Renoir")) is undoubtable. Fragonard's paintings, alongside those of [François Boucher](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boucher "François Boucher"), seem to sum up an era.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

One of Fragonard's most renowned paintings is *[The Swing](/wiki/The_Swing_(Fragonard) "The Swing (Fragonard)")*, also known as *The Happy Accidents of the Swing* (its original title), an [oil painting](/wiki/Oil_painting "Oil painting") in the [Wallace Collection](/wiki/Wallace_Collection "Wallace Collection") in London. It is considered to be one of the masterpieces of the [rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") era, and is Fragonard's best-known work.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The painting portrays a young gentleman concealed in the bushes, observing a lady on swing being pushed by her spouse, who is standing in the background, hidden in the shadows, as he is unaware of the affair. As the lady swings forward, the young man gets a glimpse under her dress.
According to [Charles Collé](/wiki/Charles_Coll%C3%A9 "Charles Collé")'s memoirs[[17]](#cite_note-colle-17) a young nobleman[[18]](#cite_note-saint-julien-18) had requested this portrait of his mistress seated on a swing. He asked first [Gabriel François Doyen](/wiki/Gabriel_Fran%C3%A7ois_Doyen "Gabriel François Doyen") to make this painting of him and his mistress. Not comfortable with this frivolous work, Doyen refused and passed on the commission to Fragonard.[[17]](#cite_note-colle-17)

*The Swing* is seemingly referenced in *[The Great Gatsby](/wiki/The_Great_Gatsby "The Great Gatsby")* by [F. Scott Fitzgerald](/wiki/F._Scott_Fitzgerald "F. Scott Fitzgerald"), in which the protagonist Nick Carraway describes an apartment "crowded to the doors with a set of tapestried furniture [... with] scenes of ladies swinging in the gardens of Versailles."[[19]](#cite_note-19) Fragonard is also referenced in [Milan Kundera](/wiki/Milan_Kundera "Milan Kundera")'s novel *Slowness*, which talks about Fragonards paintings *Progress of Love*.

The poem *The Lamentation of the Old Pensioner* by [William Butler Yeats](/wiki/W._B._Yeats "W. B. Yeats") uses the description of a broken tree and a woman that turns her face as an allusion to Fragonard's *[The Swing](/wiki/The_Swing_(Fragonard) "The Swing (Fragonard)")*,[[20]](#cite_note-20) as the branch the woman uses to swing on is broken and facing the viewer.

*[The Waste Land](/wiki/The_Waste_Land "The Waste Land")* by [T.S Eliot](/wiki/T._S._Eliot "T. S. Eliot") visually depicts the "carvéd dolphin" surmounted by winged cupids in Fragonard’s *Progress of Love: The Pursuit*.
